events.text: The patient is a 63-year-old male named Raymond Kowalski. He is a retired industrial engineer, former smoker (30 pack-year history, quit 15 years ago), with a history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus managed with lisinopril and metformin.

At age 63 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, cT3bN1M1) CLEAR CELL RENAL CELL CARCINOMA (ICD-O-3 histology code 8310/3; primary site: right kidney, ICD-O-3 site code C64.0). TNM staging: T3b (tumor extends into renal vein), N1 (regional retroperitoneal lymph node involvement), M1 (bilateral pulmonary metastases). Summary stage: Distant. Tumor size: 11.4 cm greatest dimension. Sarcomatoid differentiation: present, estimated 20% of tumor volume. Relevant diagnostic information: IMDC risk score: Intermediate risk (2 factors: <1 year from diagnosis to systemic therapy, thrombocytosis). Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY. Laterality: Right. Fuhrman/WHO-ISUP nuclear grade: Grade 4. Presenting symptoms: gross hematuria x 3 weeks, right flank pain, 15 lb unintentional weight loss over 3 months. Serum calcium: 10.9 mg/dL (slightly elevated). Hemoglobin: 10.2 g/dL (anemia). Platelet count: 478,000/uL (thrombocytosis). Neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio: 4.2. LDH: 287 U/L (mildly elevated). KPS: 80.

At age 63 years, the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (staging CT), which showed a large heterogeneous right renal mass measuring 11.4 x 9.8 x 10.1 cm with central necrosis, arising from the upper and mid pole of the right kidney; the mass extends into the right renal vein without definite IVC involvement. Multiple enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes were identified including a 2.8 cm right para-aortic node and a 1.9 cm aortocaval node. Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules consistent with metastatic disease were present, the largest measuring 1.4 cm in the right lower lobe; additional nodules 0.8 cm left lower lobe and 0.6 cm right upper lobe. No hepatic, osseous, or adrenal metastases identified. Cancer present. Metastatic sites: bilateral pulmonary nodules, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

At age 63 years, the patient had an MRI ABDOMEN WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST (pre-surgical planning), which confirmed an 11.4 cm right renal mass with involvement of the right renal vein (renal vein thrombus extending to but not into the IVC). No Gerota fascia invasion identified. The right adrenal gland appears separate from the mass. Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy confirmed. No evidence of IVC thrombus. Cancer present. Metastatic sites: right renal vein thrombus, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

At age 63 years (pre-surgical biopsy, CT-guided), the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY on a core needle biopsy specimen from the right renal mass. Diagnosis: CLEAR CELL RENAL CELL CARCINOMA, WHO/ISUP grade 4, with SARCOMATOID DIFFERENTIATION (estimated 20% of sampled tissue). Immunohistochemistry: PAX8 positive, CA-IX diffusely positive, CD10 positive, vimentin positive, CK7 focally positive, TFE3 negative. Ki-67 proliferation index: 35%. PD-L1 (SP142): Combined positive score (CPS) 15, tumor proportion score (TPS) 10%. Tumor necrosis: present.

At age 63 years, the patient had next generation sequencing performed for clear cell renal cell carcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the right renal mass (core needle biopsy). Comprehensive genomic profiling (Foundation One CDx) showed: ACTIONABLE ALTERATIONS: VHL p.L89fs*4 (frameshift deletion, pathogenic, loss of function, VAF 48%); PBRM1 p.Q357* (nonsense mutation, pathogenic, loss of function, VAF 42%); BAP1 p.R227* (nonsense mutation, pathogenic, loss of function, VAF 39%) -- presence of both PBRM1 and BAP1 loss is notable, BAP1 loss associated with higher grade and sarcomatoid features. ADDITIONAL ALTERATIONS: SETD2 p.R1625* (loss of function, VAF 31%); KDM5C p.Y611* (loss of function, VAF 22%); CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion; MET amplification (copy number 6.8); TP53 p.R248W (gain of function, VAF 18%); TERT promoter C228T mutation (VAF 35%). Copy number alterations: chromosome 3p loss (encompassing VHL, PBRM1, BAP1, SETD2 loci), chromosome 9p loss (CDKN2A/B), chromosome 14q loss, chromosome 5q gain. Tumor mutational burden: 6.2 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: MSS. No FGFR alterations. No KRAS, NRAS, or BRAF mutations detected. No ALK, ROS1, or RET fusions. PD-L1 expression (22C3): CPS 18. Relevant negative findings: no HIF2A mutation, no TSC1/TSC2 mutations, no NF2 mutation.

At age 63 years, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board (MDT) assessment. The case was reviewed by medical oncology, urology/surgical oncology, radiation oncology, and radiology. Consensus recommendation: given resectable primary tumor with limited metastatic burden (oligometastatic pulmonary disease and retroperitoneal nodal involvement), cytoreductive nephrectomy (CN) followed by systemic immunotherapy-based combination is recommended. Patient is IMDC intermediate risk. Rationale for CN: tumor burden reduction, symptom relief (hematuria, flank pain), and pathologic confirmation of sarcomatoid component. Planned systemic therapy post-CN: ipilimumab 1 mg/kg + nivolumab 3 mg/kg every 3 weeks x 4 doses, then nivolumab maintenance. Cancer present. No response assessment yet -- pre-treatment.

At age 63 years, the patient underwent CYTOREDUCTIVE RIGHT RADICAL NEPHRECTOMY WITH RETROPERITONEAL LYMPH NODE DISSECTION (open approach). Procedure: right radical nephrectomy with en bloc excision of Gerota fascia, adrenalectomy (right), and template retroperitoneal lymph node dissection. Renal vein thrombus excised. Estimated blood loss: 950 mL; 2 units pRBC transfused intraoperatively. Operative time: 4.5 hours. No intraoperative complications. Surgical margins: negative (R0 resection of primary). Lymph nodes: 8 nodes retrieved, 3 positive (3/8). Pathologic stage: ypT3bN1 (post-cytoreductive, metastatic disease remains).

At age 63 years (surgical specimen), the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY on the right radical nephrectomy specimen. Final diagnosis: CLEAR CELL RENAL CELL CARCINOMA, WHO/ISUP grade 4 (highest grade present), with SARCOMATOID DIFFERENTIATION comprising 22% of viable tumor volume (revised from biopsy estimate). Tumor size: 11.6 x 10.2 x 9.7 cm. Lymphovascular invasion: present. Perineural invasion: absent. Renal vein involvement: positive (tumor thrombus). Renal sinus fat invasion: present. Perinephric fat invasion: present. Gerota fascia: not involved. Surgical margins: all negative. Right adrenal gland: not involved by tumor. Lymph nodes: 3 of 8 retroperitoneal nodes positive for metastatic clear cell RCC with sarcomatoid features. Tumor necrosis: extensive (>50%). Immunohistochemistry on sarcomatoid component: vimentin positive, focal desmin positive, CK AE1/AE3 focally positive, PD-L1 (22C3) CPS 22 in sarcomatoid areas.

At age 63 years (4 weeks post-nephrectomy), the patient had a post-surgical oncology assessment. Recovery was unremarkable. Surgical wound healing well. Creatinine: 1.6 mg/dL (baseline pre-op 1.1; expected after nephrectomy). Hemoglobin: 10.8 g/dL (recovering). Patient reports resolution of hematuria and improvement in flank pain. Performance status: KPS 80, ECOG 1. Decision to proceed with ipilimumab 1 mg/kg + nivolumab 3 mg/kg q3 weeks x 4 cycles followed by nivolumab 480 mg q4 weeks maintenance. Patient counseled extensively on immune-related adverse events. Cancer present (pulmonary and nodal metastases remain). Pre-treatment response assessment.

At age 63 years, the patient received IPILIMUMAB 1 mg/kg + NIVOLUMAB 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks (combination immunotherapy, induction phase, 4 planned cycles) for metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma with sarcomatoid differentiation, IMDC intermediate risk, first-line setting. Treatment intent: palliative/disease control with potential for durable response. Premedications: diphenhydramine, acetaminophen. Cycle 1 administered without complications.

At age 63 years (after cycle 2 of ipilimumab/nivolumab, approximately 6 weeks into immunotherapy), the patient developed IMMUNE-RELATED ADVERSE EVENT: HYPOTHYROIDISM (grade 2, CTCAE v5.0). TSH: 18.4 mIU/L (elevated), free T4: 0.6 ng/dL (low). Patient reported fatigue and cold intolerance. Management: levothyroxine 75 mcg daily initiated; immunotherapy continued without dose modification per NCCN guidelines for grade 2 endocrinopathy. TSH trended toward normalization at 6-week follow-up. No steroids required.

At age 63 years (after completing 4 cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab induction, approximately 3 months post-initiation), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (first restaging scan). Results showed MIXED RESPONSE (RECIST 1.1 modified): Target lesions -- right lower lobe pulmonary nodule decreased from 1.4 cm to 0.9 cm (36% decrease, partial response); left lower lobe nodule stable at 0.8 cm; right upper lobe nodule decreased from 0.6 cm to 0.4 cm. Non-target lesions (retroperitoneal nodal metastases) -- previously identified right para-aortic node region (post-lymphadenectomy) showed new 1.6 cm nodal mass in the right common iliac region not previously seen; aortocaval region: 2.1 cm new nodal conglomerate. Overall assessment: mixed response -- pulmonary lesions responding, but NEW retroperitoneal/pelvic nodal disease. No new hepatic, osseous, or CNS metastases. Cancer present, mixed response to immunotherapy.

At age 63 years (3 months into immunotherapy), the patient had an oncology assessment following first restaging imaging. The mixed response pattern was discussed with the patient: improving pulmonary disease but new retroperitoneal/pelvic nodal progression. Given the pattern (possible pseudoprogression vs. true discordant response), the MDT recommendation was to continue nivolumab maintenance given responding pulmonary lesions and to repeat imaging in 6 weeks. Patient's performance status: KPS 80, ECOG 1. Patient reports mild fatigue (ongoing, grade 1) and occasional nausea (grade 1). Tolerating immunotherapy well aside from hypothyroidism (on levothyroxine). Decision: continue nivolumab 480 mg q4 weeks. Cancer present; mixed response.

At age 63 years (after 4 induction cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab), the patient transitioned to NIVOLUMAB 480 mg IV every 4 weeks (maintenance monotherapy, flat dosing) for metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma following completion of ipilimumab/nivolumab induction. Continuation of PD-1 blockade given mixed response with pulmonary improvement.

At age 63 years (approximately 6 months into immunotherapy, 6 weeks after first restaging scan), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (interim restaging). Results: Pulmonary target lesions continue to respond -- right lower lobe nodule 0.7 cm (additional decrease), right upper lobe nodule 0.3 cm. Left lower lobe nodule 0.7 cm (slightly decreased). Retroperitoneal/pelvic nodal disease: previously noted right common iliac nodal mass now 2.0 cm (increased from 1.6 cm); aortocaval conglomerate 2.4 cm (increased from 2.1 cm). New findings: 1.1 cm right obturator node. Overall: discordant response persisting -- pulmonary disease responding, nodal disease progressing. Not meeting RECIST criteria for overall progression due to pulmonary response offsetting nodal growth. Cancer present; discordant/mixed response.

At age 63 years (6 months into immunotherapy), the patient had a multidisciplinary review of ongoing discordant response. Pulmonary disease responding well to nivolumab; however nodal disease showing consistent progression. The sarcomatoid component of the tumor (22%) was felt to potentially represent an immunotherapy-resistant subclone driving the nodal progression, while the conventional clear cell component responds in the lungs. Decision: continue nivolumab for additional 6 weeks with repeat imaging; if nodal disease continues to progress, transition to cabozantinib. Patient counseled regarding potential need for treatment change. Performance status: KPS 75, ECOG 1. Cancer present; mixed response with nodal progression.

At age 63 years (approximately 9 months into immunotherapy), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (restaging, approximately 9 months post-initiation of systemic therapy). Results: Pulmonary lesions: right lower lobe 0.8 cm (stable/minimal increase from 0.7 cm); left lower lobe 0.8 cm (stable); right upper lobe 0.3 cm (stable). Retroperitoneal/pelvic nodal disease: right common iliac nodal mass 3.1 cm (increased from 2.0 cm); aortocaval conglomerate 3.4 cm (increased from 2.4 cm); right obturator node 1.8 cm (increased from 1.1 cm). New finding: right external iliac node 1.2 cm. No new pulmonary nodules. No hepatic metastases. No bone metastases. Overall RECIST assessment: PROGRESSIVE DISEASE (nodal disease sum of diameters increased >20% from nadir; overall tumor burden growing). Cancer present; disease progression on nivolumab (nodal progression).

At age 63 years (9 months after initiating first-line systemic therapy), the patient had an oncology assessment confirming RECIST progression on nivolumab maintenance. Patient has been on systemic therapy for 9 months total (ipilimumab/nivolumab x 4 cycles then nivolumab maintenance). Best response was mixed (PR in lungs, PD in nodes). Now with clear nodal progression. Patient reports new bilateral lower extremity edema (grade 1), pelvic discomfort, and worsening fatigue (grade 2). Performance status: KPS 70, ECOG 2. Labs: creatinine 1.8 mg/dL; ALT 32, AST 28 (normal); no thyroid symptoms on levothyroxine. Decision: discontinue nivolumab; initiate second-line CABOZANTINIB 60 mg orally once daily (standard starting dose for RCC). Rationale: cabozantinib targets VEGFR, MET, and AXL -- MET amplification identified on NGS, which may predict enhanced sensitivity. Patient educated on TKI side effects, particularly hand-foot syndrome, diarrhea, hypertension, and fatigue. Cancer present; progressive disease.

At age 63 years (9 months after initial diagnosis, second-line), the patient received CABOZANTINIB 60 mg orally once daily for metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma, second-line after progression on ipilimumab/nivolumab. MET amplification on NGS noted as potential biomarker of sensitivity. Treatment intent: palliative/disease control. Dose: 60 mg/day (standard). Anti-hypertensive therapy adjusted (lisinopril dose increased to 20 mg daily given anticipated TKI-related hypertension).

At age 63 years (approximately 3 weeks after initiating cabozantinib), the patient developed HAND-FOOT SYNDROME (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia, HFS; CTCAE grade 2). Symptoms: painful erythema, swelling, and blistering on bilateral palmar surfaces and plantar aspects of feet bilaterally, limiting instrumental activities of daily living. Management: cabozantinib dose reduced to 40 mg orally once daily; urea 20% cream twice daily to affected areas; gel cushion insoles recommended; patient instructed to avoid tight footwear and prolonged walking. Patient also reported DIARRHEA (CTCAE grade 2, 4-6 stools above baseline per day) beginning concurrently; managed with loperamide 4 mg initial dose then 2 mg after each loose stool (max 16 mg/day), low-residue diet counseling, and oral hydration reinforcement.

At age 63 years (5 weeks after initiating cabozantinib), the patient was evaluated for HYPERTENSION (CTCAE grade 2) with blood pressure readings 158/96 mmHg and 162/98 mmHg on two separate occasions. Patient's antihypertensive regimen augmented: amlodipine 5 mg daily added to existing lisinopril 20 mg. Blood pressure at 2-week follow-up: 142/88 mmHg (improved but not yet at goal). Amlodipine uptitrated to 10 mg daily. Cabozantinib continued at 40 mg without further dose modification. HFS improving on dose reduction and topical therapy (grade 1). Diarrhea improving (grade 1, manageable with PRN loperamide).

At age 63 years (6 weeks into cabozantinib), the patient had an oncology office visit. Patient tolerating cabozantinib 40 mg daily with manageable toxicities. HFS now grade 1 (mild erythema, no blistering). Diarrhea grade 1 (2-3 loose stools per day above baseline, managed with dietary modification and PRN loperamide). Hypertension improving on dual antihypertensive therapy. Fatigue grade 1. No hepatotoxicity (LFTs normal). TSH within normal range on levothyroxine. Performance status: KPS 75, ECOG 1 (improved from ECOG 2 at TKI initiation). Patient denies mucositis or dysgeusia. No new lower extremity edema. Cancer present (by most recent imaging); clinical response assessment pending first cabozantinib restaging scan.

At age 63 years (approximately 3 months after initiating cabozantinib, first restaging on TKI), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST. Results: Pulmonary lesions: right lower lobe nodule 0.7 cm (stable/slight decrease from 0.8 cm); left lower lobe nodule 0.7 cm (stable); right upper lobe nodule stable at 0.3 cm. Retroperitoneal/pelvic nodal disease: right common iliac nodal mass decreased from 3.1 cm to 2.3 cm; aortocaval conglomerate decreased from 3.4 cm to 2.6 cm; right obturator node decreased from 1.8 cm to 1.4 cm; right external iliac node 0.9 cm (previously 1.2 cm). Overall RECIST assessment: PARTIAL RESPONSE in nodal disease (sum of target lesion diameters decreased 27% from baseline at cabozantinib initiation). No new lesions. No hepatic, osseous, or CNS metastases. Cancer present; responding to cabozantinib.

At age 63 years (3 months into cabozantinib), the patient had an oncology assessment following first restaging scan on TKI. Excellent response to cabozantinib with partial response in the progressive nodal disease. Pulmonary disease remains stable. Patient very encouraged by response. Toxicities continue to be manageable: HFS grade 1 (well-controlled with topical therapy and dose reduction); diarrhea grade 1 (controlled with PRN loperamide and dietary changes); hypertension controlled on lisinopril 20 mg + amlodipine 10 mg (BP 138/82 today). Decision: continue cabozantinib 40 mg daily (no dose re-escalation given adequate disease control and manageable toxicities at current dose). Next restaging in 3 months. Continue levothyroxine. Cancer present; stable disease/partial response on cabozantinib.

At age 64 years (approximately 6 months after initiating cabozantinib, second restaging on TKI), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST. Results: Pulmonary lesions: right lower lobe nodule 0.7 cm (stable); left lower lobe nodule 0.7 cm (stable); right upper lobe nodule 0.3 cm (stable). Retroperitoneal/pelvic nodal disease: right common iliac nodal mass 2.2 cm (stable compared to 2.3 cm); aortocaval conglomerate 2.5 cm (stable); right obturator node 1.3 cm (stable); right external iliac node 0.8 cm (stable). No new lymphadenopathy. No new hepatic lesions. New finding: small 0.5 cm sclerotic focus in right iliac bone, indeterminate (too small to characterize, recommend bone scan). RECIST overall: STABLE DISEASE. Cancer present; stable disease on cabozantinib.

At age 64 years (bone scan following indeterminate iliac bone finding on CT), the patient had a WHOLE-BODY TECHNETIUM-99M BONE SCAN. Results: No convincing evidence of osteoblastic or osteolytic metastatic disease. The right iliac sclerotic focus corresponds to an area of mildly increased radiotracer uptake, likely reactive/degenerative etiology rather than metastatic disease. No other areas of abnormal uptake identified. Conclusion: No definitive bone metastases. Cancer status: no new metastatic sites confirmed.

At age 64 years (6 months into cabozantinib), the patient had an oncology assessment following second restaging scans on TKI therapy. Stable disease confirmed on cabozantinib 40 mg daily. Bone scan reassuring -- no osseous metastases. Patient's overall disease status: stable pulmonary and nodal disease. Performance status: KPS 80, ECOG 1. Current toxicities: HFS grade 1 (chronic, manageable with ongoing topical therapy); diarrhea grade 1 (2 loose stools per day above baseline, well-managed); hypertension controlled (BP 136/80 today on current regimen). No significant fatigue, mucositis, or hepatotoxicity. Patient is working part-time and maintaining quality of life. Labs: creatinine 1.9 mg/dL (stable); normal LFTs; TSH 2.8 mIU/L (therapeutic). Plan: continue cabozantinib 40 mg daily; maintain current supportive medications; restage in 3 months; continue monitoring for emerging resistance or new metastatic sites. Cancer present; stable disease -- best response to second-line cabozantinib.

At age 64 years (approximately 9 months after initiating cabozantinib; 18 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had a routine oncology office visit. Patient reports he has been tolerating cabozantinib 40 mg daily continuously. Reports chronic grade 1 HFS requiring daily topical urea cream and careful foot care; intermittent grade 1 diarrhea managed with dietary adjustments. Voice slight dysgeusia (grade 1 taste changes) that has been present for several months, affecting appetite but not causing significant weight loss (weight stable at 178 lbs, down from 198 lbs at diagnosis). Blood pressure well-controlled. No new symptoms. ECOG performance status: 1. Labs: CBC, CMP, LFTs within normal limits (creatinine 1.8 mg/dL stable). Plan: proceed with CT restaging as scheduled. Continue current cabozantinib dose and supportive measures. Quality of life acceptable; patient engaged with survivorship counseling. Cancer present; ongoing stable disease assessment pending.

documents.text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
RESTAGING EXAMINATION -- FOLLOWING 4 CYCLES OF Yervoy/Opdivo INDUCTION

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Raymond Kowalski, 63-year-old male with known Stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (right kidney primary, WHO/ISUP grade 4, with sarcomatoid differentiation), status post cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection. The patient subsequently initiated combination immunotherapy with ipi 1 mg/kg and nivo 3 mg/kg every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles for residual bilateral pulmonary metastases and retroperitoneal nodal disease. This examination is performed for routine restaging following completion of the induction phase, approximately 3 months after systemic therapy initiation. Reference prior examinations: staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (initial presentation) and post-surgical CT obtained prior to immunotherapy initiation.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 100 mL of non-ionic iodinated intravenous contrast material (iohexol). Acquisitions were obtained in the portal venous phase. Coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformations were reviewed in conjunction with axial images. Slice thickness: 3 mm (chest), 3 mm (abdomen/pelvis). No enteric contrast administered. Images are compared to the prior staging CT and the pre-therapy MRI abdomen.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
FINDINGS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways:
The airways are patent to the segmental level bilaterally. There is no endobronchial lesion identified. The lungs are otherwise clear without consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or pleural-based nodularity to suggest immunotherapy-related pneumonitis. Specifically, no diffuse or patchy ground-glass opacities are appreciated in the perihilar or peripheral lung zones. No air trapping or mosaic attenuation pattern.

Pulmonary Nodules (Previously Documented Metastatic Lesions):
-- Right lower lobe: A previously documented nodule in the posterior basal segment of the right lower lobe has decreased in size from 1.4 cm to 0.9 cm in greatest axial dimension. The nodule retains its rounded morphology with smooth margins; it now appears less conspicuous on lung windows. This represents a meaningful decrease in size compared to the staging examination.
-- Left lower lobe: A previously noted nodule in the posterior basal segment of the left lower lobe remains grossly stable, measuring 0.8 cm (unchanged from prior). The nodule maintains rounded margins without internal change.
-- Right upper lobe: A previously documented small nodule in the posterior segment of the right upper lobe has decreased from 0.6 cm to approximately 0.4 cm in greatest dimension and is now borderline measurable on this examination. No new nodules are identified in the right upper lobe.
-- No new pulmonary nodules are identified. No cavitating lesions. No satellite nodules surrounding the known lesions.

Pleura:
No pleural effusion bilaterally. No pneumothorax. No pleural thickening or pleural-based masses.

Mediastinum and Hila:
The heart is normal in size without pericardial effusion. The great vessels are unremarkable. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy above the threshold for enlargement. The trachea is midline. The esophagus appears normal in caliber.

Chest Wall:
No chest wall masses or rib destruction. No axillary lymphadenopathy.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ABDOMEN:

Liver:
The liver is normal in size with homogeneous attenuation. No focal hepatic lesion identified. The portal and hepatic veins are patent. The biliary system is not dilated. No periportal edema.

Gallbladder and Bile Ducts:
The gallbladder is present without calculi, wall thickening, or pericholecystic fluid. The common bile duct is not dilated.

Pancreas:
The pancreas is normal in size and attenuation with no ductal dilation, focal mass, or peripancreatic stranding.

Spleen:
The spleen is normal in size and homogeneous in attenuation. No splenic lesions.

Adrenal Glands:
The left adrenal gland appears normal. The right adrenal gland is absent, consistent with prior right adrenalectomy performed at the time of cytoreductive nephrectomy.

Kidneys:
The right renal fossa is postoperative in appearance, consistent with prior right radical nephrectomy. There is expected postoperative soft tissue stranding and small volume fluid in the right renal fossa without a discrete mass to suggest local recurrence at this time. The left kidney is present with normal size, contour, and parenchymal enhancement. No left-sided hydronephrosis or renal mass identified.

Retroperitoneal and Mesenteric Lymph Nodes:
In the upper retroperitoneum, within the aortocaval region, there is a lobular soft tissue nodal conglomerate measuring approximately 2.1 cm in short-axis dimension in the upper para-aortic/aortocaval region. This represents NEW nodal disease compared to the prior staging examination; this region was surgically sampled and was not described as a discrete residual nodal mass on the post-nephrectomy imaging. The nodule has rounded contours with slightly heterogeneous enhancement. The right para-aortic surgical bed demonstrates postoperative change without a clearly defined residual nodal mass in the exact location previously dissected, though the distinction from postoperative fibrosis is limited.

Gastrointestinal:
The stomach, small bowel, and large bowel are unremarkable without wall thickening, obstruction, or inflammatory change. No free intraperitoneal air or fluid.

Aorta and Vessels:
The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber. The celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, and renal arteries are patent. The inferior vena cava is patent without filling defect.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

PELVIS:

Pelvic Lymph Nodes:
A rounded, well-defined soft tissue nodal mass measuring approximately 1.6 cm in greatest dimension is identified along the right common iliac chain. This nodule demonstrates intermediate attenuation with mild heterogeneous post-contrast enhancement. This lymph node was NOT present on prior staging imaging and represents new nodal disease in the pelvis not previously documented. The left iliac chain and inguinal regions appear unremarkable without pathologically enlarged nodes.

Pelvic Organs:
The bladder is normally distended without wall thickening or intraluminal filling defect. The prostate gland is mildly enlarged, consistent with benign prostatic enlargement, without a focal lesion. The seminal vesicles appear symmetric and unremarkable. No free pelvic fluid.

Bone Windows:
The imaged osseous structures, including the lumbar spine, sacrum, iliac bones, and proximal femora, demonstrate no lytic or sclerotic lesions to suggest skeletal metastasis. No pathologic fractures are identified. Multilevel degenerative changes are present in the lumbar spine consistent with the patient's age.

Soft Tissue:
No suspicious soft tissue masses are identified in the imaged regions. No subcutaneous nodules.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
IMPRESSION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. DISCORDANT/MIXED RESPONSE TO IMMUNOTHERAPY:
   The pulmonary metastatic nodules show a favorable response since initiation of Yervoy/Opdivo. The right lower lobe nodule has appreciably decreased from 1.4 cm to 0.9 cm, and the right upper lobe nodule has also decreased from 0.6 cm to approximately 0.4 cm. The left lower lobe nodule is stable. No new pulmonary metastases are identified. The pulmonary disease is clearly responding to the current immunotherapy regimen.

2. NEW RETROPERITONEAL NODAL DISEASE:
   There is a new 2.1 cm aortocaval nodal conglomerate in the upper retroperitoneum not present on prior imaging. This represents emergence of nodal disease in this region, which could reflect either true progression of nodal metastatic disease in the surgical bed, or possibly an unusual response pattern. Given the heterogeneous enhancement pattern, metastatic involvement is favored over inflammatory/reactive etiology.

3. NEW PELVIC NODAL DISEASE:
   A new 1.6 cm right common iliac chain lymph node has appeared since the staging examination. This was not present on any prior imaging and represents new disease in a nodal station not previously involved. The morphology and enhancement characteristics are consistent with nodal metastatic involvement in the setting of this patient's known metastatic clear cell RCC.

4. No evidence of hepatic, osseous (within imaged skeleton), or central nervous system (not imaged) metastases. No adrenal metastasis in the retained left adrenal gland. The right renal fossa does not demonstrate convincing local recurrence.

5. No imaging features of immune-related pneumonitis, colitis, or other immune-mediated organ toxicity.

Summary: This examination documents a mixed response pattern -- the bilateral pulmonary nodules are decreasing in size and responding to Yervoy/nivo, while new retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal lesions have emerged. Clinical and oncologic correlation with multidisciplinary discussion is warranted to determine whether this pattern represents pseudoprogression, discordant true response, or nodal disease progression in the setting of pulmonary response.

MEDICAL ONCOLOGY -- FOLLOW-UP VISIT NOTE
Patient: Raymond Kowalski
DOB/Age: 63-year-old male
Diagnosis: Stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma with sarcomatoid differentiation (22%), right kidney primary (pT3bN1M1), bilateral pulmonary metastases and retroperitoneal/pelvic nodal metastases
Visit Type: Scheduled oncology follow-up -- first restaging assessment after completing ipi/nivo induction
Attending: Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Kowalski is a 63-year-old male with metastatic clear cell RCC presenting for scheduled follow-up after completing 4 cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab induction immunotherapy and review of first restaging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski was diagnosed with stage IV (cT3bN1M1) clear cell renal cell carcinoma of the right kidney approximately 3 months ago. He presented initially with gross hematuria, right flank pain, and a 15-pound unintentional weight loss. Staging workup demonstrated an 11.4 cm right renal mass with extension of tumor thrombus into the right renal vein, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (right para-aortic and aortocaval nodes), and bilateral pulmonary nodules. He was determined to be IMDC intermediate risk (2 risk factors: time from diagnosis to systemic therapy less than one year, thrombocytosis). Pathology from both the pre-surgical core needle biopsy and the surgical specimen confirmed WHO/ISUP grade 4 clear cell RCC with 22% sarcomatoid differentiation. Comprehensive genomic profiling (FoundationOne CDx) demonstrated VHL loss-of-function mutation, PBRM1 loss, BAP1 loss (the co-occurrence of BAP1 and PBRM1 loss being notable for its association with higher-grade biology and sarcomatoid features), SETD2 loss, MET amplification, CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion, and TP53 R248W gain-of-function mutation, among other alterations. PD-L1 (22C3) CPS was 18 on the core biopsy and CPS 22 in sarcomatoid areas on the nephrectomy specimen.

Given his resectable primary with oligometastatic disease, he underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection approximately 7 weeks prior to his first oncology visit post-surgery. Pathologic staging confirmed pT3b (renal vein thrombus), with 3 of 8 retroperitoneal nodes positive. He recovered well from surgery with resolution of hematuria and improvement of flank pain. Creatinine settled at approximately 1.6 mg/dL post-nephrectomy (from a baseline of 1.1 mg/dL).

Approximately 4 weeks post-nephrectomy, he was started on ipilimumab 1 mg/kg + nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks. He completed 4 induction cycles over approximately 12 weeks without significant immune-related toxicity, with the notable exception of grade 2 immune-related hypothyroidism that developed after cycle 2. TSH was 18.4 mIU/L with a suppressed free T4 of 0.6 ng/dL; he was started on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Repeat TSH 6 weeks later trended toward normalization. Immunotherapy was continued through all 4 cycles without dose modification.

He now presents for restaging review. The CT chest/abdomen/pelvis obtained this week demonstrated a mixed response: the right lower lobe pulmonary target lesion decreased from 1.4 cm to 0.9 cm (approximately 36% reduction), and the right upper lobe nodule decreased from 0.6 cm to 0.4 cm -- both clearly smaller. The left lower lobe nodule is stable at 0.8 cm. However, the retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal compartments show new or growing disease: a 1.6 cm right common iliac nodal mass not seen on prior imaging, and a new 2.1 cm aortocaval nodal conglomerate (the prior individual para-aortic and aortocaval nodes measured 2.8 and 1.9 cm, but those were resected at the time of retroperitoneal lymph node dissection -- the new aortocaval conglomerate therefore represents recurrence/new nodal disease in the resected field). No hepatic, bone, or brain metastases are identified.

This discordant response -- pulmonary disease clearly improving while new nodal disease has appeared -- was discussed at length with the patient today. The differential for this pattern in the context of immunotherapy includes: (1) true discordant response, with immunotherapy effectively controlling the conventional clear cell component (which predominates in the lungs) but the sarcomatoid subclone driving nodal disease and proving less immunotherapy-sensitive in these nodal sites; (2) pseudoprogression, where immune infiltration into nodal metastases transiently enlarges them before tumor cell death; (3) early true progression at the nodal sites despite pulmonary benefit. Given that this is the first restaging scan, that pulmonary disease is clearly and meaningfully responding, and that the patient remains clinically well, the MDT consensus is to proceed cautiously before abandoning immunotherapy -- particularly given the established principle that checkpoint inhibitor responses can be heterogeneous, and abandoning effective therapy prematurely would be detrimental.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage IV clear cell RCC with sarcomatoid differentiation, on nivolumab maintenance (cycle 1 of maintenance dosing, transitioning from ipi/nivo induction): MIXED RESPONSE on first restaging imaging.

The radiographic picture today is complex and warrants careful consideration. The pulmonary lesions have clearly responded to immunotherapy -- both the right lower lobe and right upper lobe nodules are measurably smaller, and this change exceeds what we would attribute to measurement variability. The left lower lobe nodule is stable. By standard criteria, the pulmonary component is in partial response.

However, new nodal disease has appeared in the right common iliac and aortocaval regions. The nature of this discordance is biologically plausible in the context of this particular tumor: his surgical specimen showed that 22% of viable tumor volume was sarcomatoid differentiation, and the sarcomatoid component had particularly high PD-L1 expression (CPS 22). While high PD-L1 is generally associated with immunotherapy benefit, sarcomatoid differentiation is also known to be associated with more aggressive biology and potentially heterogeneous immunotherapy responses. His NGS profile included both BAP1 and PBRM1 loss -- BAP1 loss in particular is associated with higher-grade tumors and aggressive natural history. It is possible that the conventional clear cell clones are responding in the lung while a genomically distinct clone is driving nodal progression.

Differential for this pattern:
- Pseudoprogression (immune infiltration causing transient enlargement of nodal metastases; the new nodes may regress on continued therapy)
- True discordant response (pulmonary disease benefiting; nodal disease progressing)
- Delayed immune response (nodes may ultimately respond; pulmonary benefit may be early signal of broader response)

(a) This is the first restaging scan and we have not yet allowed adequate time to assess the full scope of the immunotherapy response;
(b) The pulmonary disease is clearly responding, which is encouraging and warrants continued anti-PD-1 therapy if safely possible;
(c) The patient is clinically stable with ECOG 1 performance status;
(d) The nodal disease, while new, is not causing critical organ compromise or rapid clinical deterioration;

The aortocaval nodal conglomerate, which was 2.1 cm at first restaging, is now 2.4 cm. A new 1.1 cm right obturator node is identified that was not present on prior scans. ASSESSMENT

Mr. Kowalski is a 63-year-old male with stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma, WHO/ISUP grade 4 with 22% sarcomatoid differentiation, IMDC intermediate risk, now approximately 6 months into first-line systemic therapy. His disease has manifested a persistently discordant response pattern that has become clearer over successive imaging: ongoing and deepening response in the pulmonary metastases (now >50% reduction from baseline in the dominant right lower lobe nodule), in the setting of concurrent and progressive nodal disease in the retroperitoneum and pelvis with expansion to a third nodal station (right obturator).

The biology of this discordant pattern merits discussion. The tumor carries co-loss of PBRM1 and BAP1, which is an uncommon and molecularly significant finding in ccRCC. BAP1-deficient tumors tend to be higher grade, more aggressive, and often have the sarcomatoid features seen here. PBRM1 and BAP1 mutations are thought to define distinct molecular subtypes of ccRCC with different immune microenvironments and possibly different sensitivities to immunotherapy. It is plausible that the conventional clear cell component (predominant in the pulmonary deposits) is responding to PD-1 blockade, while the sarcomatoid/BAP1-deficient subclone may be driving the nodal progression. This heterogeneity between metastatic sites has been observed in the literature and is a recognized challenge in managing sarcomatoid RCC.

From a clinical standpoint, the nodal progression is now clear and consecutive across two imaging timepoints over 6 weeks, including emergence of a new nodal station. This is no longer a diagnostic ambiguity regarding pseudoprogression -- the nodes are consistently, directionally growing.

The case was reviewed today with the multidisciplinary team. Radiation oncology was consulted regarding possible stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) to the most prominent pelvic/retroperitoneal nodes in combination with continued systemic therapy, but given the multi-station, multi-node involvement (now 4 distinct nodes/nodal masses in 3 anatomic regions), this approach was felt to be not feasible for comprehensive control. Continuing Opdivo alone without modification -- given the established and ongoing nodal progression at this point -- is no longer supportable as the sole strategy.

MDT consensus recommendation: Continue nivo for an additional 6 weeks with a repeat CT at that time. If the nodal disease continues to grow, transition to second-line cabozantinib. Rationale for this approach: (1) the pulmonary response on Opdivo is meaningful and durable, and abrupt discontinuation may lead to pulmonary rebound; (2) cabozantinib's activity is well-established in second-line ccRCC (METEOR trial data) and has particular mechanistic rationale given the patient's MET amplification on NGS (cabozantinib targets VEGFR2, MET, and AXL); (3) another short observation window is acceptable given the patient's maintained ECOG 1 status, absence of symptoms directly threatening end-organ function, and the fact that nodal progression, while clear, is not yet causing significant lymphedema or ureteral obstruction.

The patient was counseled in detail about this recommendation, including the high likelihood that cabozantinib will be needed at the next assessment based on current trajectory. He understands and agrees with the plan. He expressed concern about the discordant nature of his response and was reassured that this pattern, while unusual, is recognized in sarcomatoid RCC treated with immunotherapy, and that cabozantinib has demonstrated responses in this setting.

Mr. Kowalski is a 63-year-old male with stage IV clear cell RCC, WHO/ISUP grade 4 with sarcomatoid differentiation (22%), who has experienced a discordant response to first-line ipilimumab/nivolumab induction followed by nivolumab maintenance. His LDH has modestly risen, and his performance status has declined from ECOG 1 to ECOG 2, driven largely by fatigue and lower extremity edema.

This represents radiographic and clinical progression on PD-1 checkpoint blockade therapy. He has received a total of approximately 9 months of immunotherapy.

1. DISEASE STATUS -- PROGRESSIVE DISEASE ON FIRST-LINE IMMUNOTHERAPY:
We have confirmed progression on nivolumab maintenance. Given the consistent pattern of nodal growth over multiple scans (3 imaging time points showing progressive nodal enlargement, new nodal sites), this is definitively true progression, not pseudoprogression. Notably, his sarcomatoid dedifferentiation (22% at surgical specimen, CPS 22 by 22C3 in sarcomatoid areas) is a double-edged feature: it can initially respond to immunotherapy but can also be a source of acquired resistance, particularly when driven by BAP1 loss (which this patient has) and CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion, both of which are associated with aggressive tumor biology.

2. TRANSITION TO SECOND-LINE THERAPY -- CABOZANTINIB:
After review and discussion with the patient and his wife, we will discontinue nivolumab and initiate second-line therapy with cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 60 mg orally once daily as standard starting dose.

Rationale for cabozantinib as second-line choice:
- Cabozantinib is a multi-kinase inhibitor with activity against VEGFR1/2/3, MET, AXL, RET, and other targets. The METEOR trial established its superiority over everolimus in previously treated ccRCC with significant improvement in OS, PFS, and ORR.
- Critically for this patient, his NGS profile showed MET amplification (copy number 6.8). Cabozantinib directly targets MET kinase, and preclinical and clinical data suggest that MET-amplified tumors may be particularly sensitive to MET inhibition. This patient's nodal progression may be partly MET-driven, and cabozantinib is well-positioned to address this.
- AXL overexpression has been associated with sarcomatoid differentiation in RCC, and cabozantinib's AXL inhibitory activity makes it particularly attractive in this context.
- Given that the pulmonary disease responded to immunotherapy and there is residual immune benefit at pulmonary sites, we are choosing a VEGFR TKI rather than a combination approach at this time. Cabozantinib monotherapy is the appropriate standard second-line option.

Starting dose: 60 mg/day. Dose reductions to 40 mg/day and then 20 mg/day available for toxicity management per label.

Key side effects reviewed extensively with patient and his wife:
- Hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia): Recommend starting urea 20% or lactic acid cream prophylactically; avoid tight footwear; inspect hands and feet daily; return immediately if blistering or inability to walk.
- Diarrhea: Begin loperamide at first sign of loose stools (4 mg initial dose, 2 mg after each loose stool); low-residue/BRAT diet guidance provided.
- Hypertension: He is already on lisinopril for baseline hypertension and his BP has been reasonably controlled. TKIs frequently cause or worsen hypertension through VEGF inhibition. We will increase lisinopril to 20 mg daily preemptively and monitor BPs closely (home BP log requested). He should call if BP consistently above 150/90.
- Fatigue and nausea: Common. Fatigue may overlap with his disease-related fatigue. Nausea usually mild; PRN antiemetics prescribed (ondansetron 4 mg as needed).
- Hypothyroidism: He already has immune-related hypothyroidism on levothyroxine. TKIs can also cause hypothyroidism (separate mechanism via thyroid inflammation/ischemia). Will monitor TSH monthly initially.
- Hepatotoxicity: LFTs will be monitored at each visit.
- Wound healing: No active surgical wounds. Patient has healed nephrectomy site, so not an immediate concern.
- QTc prolongation: Baseline EKG to be obtained today before first dose.
- Proteinuria: Urine protein/creatinine ratio to be checked at next visit.

Prescription provided for cabozantinib 60 mg (Cabometyx). Patient and family given written educational materials. Pharmacy contact information provided for questions.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski was originally diagnosed with Stage IV (cT3bN1M1) clear cell RCC of the right kidney approximately 18 months ago. At presentation, he had an 11.4 cm right renal mass with extension into the right renal vein, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy (right para-aortic and aortocaval nodes), and bilateral pulmonary metastases. Genomic profiling (Foundation One CDx) demonstrated VHL frameshift, PBRM1 and BAP1 co-loss (the latter associated with his higher grade and sarcomatoid features), SETD2 loss, CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion, MET amplification, and TP53 gain-of-function mutation -- a genomically complex tumor. He is IMDC intermediate risk (2 factors: <1 year from diagnosis to systemic therapy, thrombocytosis at presentation).

He underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection approximately 15 months ago, with R0 resection of the primary and 3 of 8 retroperitoneal nodes positive for metastatic disease with sarcomatoid features. Sarcomatoid differentiation was revised to 22% of viable tumor volume on final nephrectomy specimen.

He then received first-line ipilimumab (1 mg/kg) plus nivolumab (3 mg/kg) every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles, followed by nivolumab 480 mg every 4 weeks as maintenance. His course was complicated by immune-related hypothyroidism (grade 2) after cycle 2, managed with levothyroxine without need for steroid therapy or immunotherapy interruption. On restaging imaging, he demonstrated a discordant/mixed response: his pulmonary metastases showed clear improvement (the dominant right lower lobe nodule decreased from 1.4 cm to a nadir of approximately 0.7 cm), but he developed new and progressive retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal disease over the ensuing months. This pattern was felt to possibly reflect differential sensitivity between the conventional clear cell and the sarcomatoid components of his tumor. Despite a period of continued observation given the pulmonary response, progressive nodal disease was confirmed at approximately 9 months from initiation of systemic therapy, meeting criteria for overall disease progression on nivolumab.

He was transitioned to second-line cabozantinib, initiated at 60 mg daily. Within the first 3 weeks of cabozantinib, he developed grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (HFS) and grade 2 diarrhea, requiring dose reduction to 40 mg daily and initiation of supportive measures. He also developed grade 2 hypertension at approximately 5 weeks, requiring augmentation of his antihypertensive regimen with addition of amlodipine to his pre-existing lisinopril, which had already been dose-escalated in anticipation of TKI-related hypertension.

On first restaging at 3 months of cabozantinib (40 mg), he achieved a partial response in his nodal disease (approximately 27% reduction in sum of target nodal diameters). At 6 months of TKI therapy, his most recent full restaging showed stable disease overall, with all nodal and pulmonary disease remaining stable. A small, indeterminate sclerotic focus in the right iliac bone noted on that CT was subsequently assessed by whole-body bone scan, which was reassuring -- the focus was felt to be most consistent with a reactive/degenerative process without convincing evidence of osteoblastic or osteolytic metastatic disease.

He now presents for a scheduled routine visit at approximately 9 months of cabozantinib therapy. He has been on the drug continuously at the 40 mg daily dose since the dose reduction. ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Impression: Mr. Kowalski is a 64-year-old gentleman with metastatic clear cell RCC with sarcomatoid features, progressed after cytoreductive nephrectomy and ipilimumab/nivolumab-based first-line therapy, now on second-line cabozantinib for approximately 9 months. His performance status has improved back to ECOG 1 and he continues to work part-time and maintain quality of life. This is an encouraging trajectory.

1. Metastatic clear cell RCC with sarcomatoid differentiation -- STABLE DISEASE on cabozantinib 40 mg daily.
   - Patient and I discussed his overall disease course and current status at length. He understands that his disease remains metastatic and not curable with current therapy, but that the goal is to maintain disease control and quality of life for as long as possible.
   - Continue cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily. No dose modification warranted given stable disease with manageable toxicity profile at current dose. I did not re-escalate to 60 mg given that we achieved excellent disease control at 40 mg and his toxicities, while managed, would be expected to worsen with dose increase.
   - Proceed with CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for third restaging on cabozantinib (approximately 9 months of TKI). I have ordered this scan to be completed within the next 1-2 weeks and we will review results at his next appointment or via portal message if results come in prior to scheduled follow-up.
   - If at next restaging we see disease progression on cabozantinib, the discussion will shift toward third-line options. Potential options would include everolimus or lenvatinib/everolimus combination, or consideration of a clinical trial. His MET amplification and complex genomic landscape may also make him a candidate for investigational options if available.

NUCLEAR MEDICINE REPORT
WHOLE-BODY BONE SCINTIGRAPHY (TECHNETIUM-99m METHYLENE DIPHOSPHONATE)

INDICATION:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 64-year-old male with known metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (right kidney, pT3bN1M1) with sarcomatoid differentiation (22%), status post cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection. He has been receiving cabozantinib 40 mg orally once daily as second-line therapy following progression on ipilimumab/nivolumab. Recent surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis demonstrated stable pulmonary and retroperitoneal/pelvic nodal disease with a new indeterminate 0.5 cm sclerotic focus in the right iliac wing, too small to definitively characterize on cross-sectional imaging. This bone scan is obtained to evaluate for the possibility of osseous metastatic disease.

RADIOPHARMACEUTICAL AND TECHNIQUE:
Intravenous injection of 25.1 mCi (930 MBq) of technetium-99m methylene diphosphonate (Tc-99m MDP) was administered without adverse reaction. Planar whole-body imaging was performed in anterior and posterior projections from vertex to toes at approximately 3 hours post-injection on a dual-head gamma camera. Supplemental spot views of the pelvis, lumbar spine, and bilateral lower extremities were obtained given the clinical question regarding the right iliac bone. SPECT/CT images of the pelvis and lower lumbar spine were additionally acquired and reconstructed for anatomic correlation of areas of interest.

COMPARISON:
Prior CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (most recent, approximately 6 months post-cabozantinib initiation).

FINDINGS:

SKULL AND CERVICAL SPINE:
No focal areas of abnormal radiotracer uptake are identified in the calvarium, skull base, or cervical spine. Symmetric and homogeneous tracer distribution in the visualized cranial and upper axial skeleton.

THORACIC SKELETON:
Symmetric radiotracer uptake throughout the ribs bilaterally. No discrete focal abnormality to suggest cortical disruption or blastic/lytic metastatic lesion. Mild, bilaterally symmetric uptake at multiple costo-chondral junctions is noted, a nonspecific but common degenerative finding. The thoracic vertebral bodies demonstrate uniform tracer activity without focal photopenic or hyperactive lesion. No sternal or clavicular abnormality.

LUMBAR SPINE:
Uniform radiotracer uptake in the lumbar vertebral bodies from L1 through L5. No focal lesion identified. There is mild, diffusely increased uptake at the L4-L5 and L5-S1 facet joints bilaterally, consistent with degenerative facet arthropathy and in keeping with the patient's age. This pattern is symmetric and does not suggest metastatic involvement.

PELVIS AND SACRUM:
The sacrum demonstrates homogeneous, symmetric radiotracer uptake. The sacroiliac joints show mildly increased but symmetric activity bilaterally, consistent with degenerative changes.

Right iliac wing: On the planar images, there is a subtle focus of mildly increased radiotracer uptake projecting over the right iliac wing, corresponding in location to the 0.5 cm sclerotic focus identified on the prior CT. This area of uptake is discrete but low-grade in intensity -- it does not demonstrate the brisk, intense radiotracer avidity that is typically associated with aggressive osteoblastic metastatic disease. The degree of uptake is only modestly above background and symmetric regional bone marrow activity. On SPECT/CT correlation, this focus localizes to a small area of cortical sclerosis in the right iliac wing without associated soft tissue mass, periosteal reaction, or cortical destruction. The morphologic and scintigraphic appearance is most consistent with a benign process such as a bone island (enostosis) or reactive/degenerative sclerosis, rather than a metastatic deposit. There is no photopenic center within the focus that would suggest central necrosis characteristic of a large metastasis.

Left iliac wing, ischium, and pubic rami bilaterally: No focal abnormality. Bilateral acetabular regions show symmetric uptake without discrete lesion.

FEMORA AND LOWER EXTREMITIES:
Normal radiotracer distribution throughout the bilateral proximal and mid femora. The femoral heads and necks are symmetric in uptake. The bilateral knees show mild periarticular uptake consistent with mild degenerative joint disease. Tibiae, fibulae, and feet demonstrate uniform tracer distribution. No focal lesion suggestive of osseous metastasis in the visualized lower extremities.

UPPER EXTREMITIES:
Symmetric radiotracer distribution in the bilateral humeri, radii, ulnae, and hands. No focal abnormality identified.

KIDNEY AND SOFT TISSUES:
As expected in the setting of prior right nephrectomy, there is absent tracer excretion from the right renal fossa. The left kidney demonstrates normal tracer uptake and excretion. Background soft tissue activity is within normal limits for the administered dose and timing. There is no focal soft tissue uptake to suggest extraosseous metastatic disease or sites of dystrophic calcification.

BLADDER:
The urinary bladder contains radiotracer representing physiologic renal excretion of the radiopharmaceutical. No pelvic soft tissue abnormality is scintigraphically evident.

IMPRESSION:

1. SINGLE FOCUS OF MILDLY INCREASED UPTAKE IN THE RIGHT ILIAC WING, corresponding to the 0.5 cm sclerotic lesion noted on recent CT. The scintigraphic characteristics of this focus -- low-grade intensity, absence of a photopenic center, and absence of cortical destruction or periosteal reaction on SPECT/CT correlation -- are most in keeping with a benign etiology such as a bone island or focal reactive sclerosis. There is no convincing scintigraphic evidence of an aggressive or metastatic process at this site. This appearance is not typical of renal cell carcinoma osseous metastasis, which tends to be lytic or photopenic rather than densely sclerotic.

2. NO ADDITIONAL FOCAL AREAS OF ABNORMAL RADIOTRACER UPTAKE are identified in the axial or appendicular skeleton to suggest osseous metastatic disease elsewhere. In particular, there are no photopenic lesions, no areas of intense focal hyperactivity in a pattern consistent with osteoblastic metastases, and no evidence of the "superscan" pattern.

3. DEGENERATIVE CHANGES involving the lumbar facet joints, bilateral sacroiliac joints, and bilateral knees are noted as an incidental finding consistent with the patient's age and prior history.

4. ABSENT RIGHT RENAL UPTAKE consistent with prior right nephrectomy.

Overall, this whole-body bone scan does not demonstrate convincing evidence of osseous metastatic involvement. The indeterminate right iliac sclerotic focus identified on the prior CT most likely represents a benign bone island or focal reactive change; continued surveillance with cross-sectional imaging is appropriate if there is any interval change in appearance.

Approximately 4 weeks post-nephrectomy, he initiated first-line systemic therapy with ipi 1 mg/kg plus Opdivo 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles, followed by transition to Opdivo 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance. He tolerated the induction regimen reasonably well; his principal immunotherapy-related toxicity was grade 2 hypothyroidism developing after cycle 2, managed with levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, which has remained a chronic medication requirement.

This discordant pattern raised initial consideration of pseudoprogression, but on two subsequent scans over the next three months, nodal disease continued to grow (right common iliac node enlarging to 3.1 cm, aortocaval conglomerate to 3.4 cm, new right external iliac node) while pulmonary lesions remained stable. After discussion at multidisciplinary review, this was attributed to differential biology between the responding conventional clear cell component (pulmonary sites) and a potentially immunotherapy-resistant sarcomatoid-predominant subclone driving nodal progression. Opdivo was discontinued at the 9-month mark from initial systemic therapy, and the patient was transitioned to second-line cabozantinib given the MET amplification on NGS and its VEGFR/MET/AXL inhibitory profile.

He also developed grade 2 hypertension requiring addition of amlodipine 5 mg, subsequently uptitrated to 10 mg, to his existing lisinopril 20 mg. These toxicities stabilized over the ensuing weeks: HFS improved to grade 1, diarrhea to grade 1, and blood pressure came under better control.

Of note, a new 0.5 cm sclerotic focus in the right iliac bone was identified on CT, but a confirmatory whole-body technetium bone scan showed no convincing evidence of osseous metastatic disease; the focus was attributed to a reactive/degenerative etiology. MRI ABDOMEN WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 63-year-old male with recently diagnosed right renal cell carcinoma. Staging CT demonstrated a large right renal mass with suspected renal vein involvement without definitive IVC extension, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, and bilateral pulmonary nodules consistent with metastatic disease. MRI requested for surgical planning, specifically to better characterize the extent of venous tumor thrombus and assess for Gerota fascia involvement prior to planned cytoreductive nephrectomy.

TECHNIQUE: Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the abdomen performed on a 3T magnet. Sequences include T1-weighted in-phase and out-of-phase imaging, T2-weighted fat-suppressed axial and coronal sequences, diffusion-weighted imaging with ADC mapping, and dynamic contrast-enhanced imaging following intravenous administration of gadolinium-based contrast agent (gadoteridol, 0.1 mmol/kg), obtained in the corticomedullary, nephrographic, and excretory phases. Dedicated thin-slice coronal T2 and VIBE post-contrast sequences were obtained through the renal veins and inferior vena cava for vascular assessment.

COMPARISON: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast performed approximately one week prior.

FINDINGS:

RIGHT KIDNEY AND PRIMARY TUMOR:
There is a large, predominantly exophytic mass arising from the upper and mid pole of the right kidney measuring approximately 11.4 x 10.2 x 9.8 cm in craniocaudal, transverse, and anteroposterior dimensions, consistent with the primary renal cell carcinoma identified on prior CT. The mass is markedly heterogeneous on all sequences, exhibiting areas of avid early arterial enhancement along its peripheral viable rim with progressive washout on delayed phases, behavior characteristic of clear cell renal cell carcinoma. Extensive central areas demonstrate markedly low T1 and T2 signal with absent enhancement, in keeping with the geographic coagulative necrosis noted on biopsy. There are also scattered foci of T1 hyperintensity within the mass, likely representing areas of intratumoral hemorrhage or proteinaceous content. On diffusion-weighted imaging, viable peripheral tumor demonstrates restricted diffusion with low ADC values, consistent with hypercellular viable tumor. A minority of internal areas with heterogeneous but intermediate ADC values raise the possibility of areas with divergent histologic features (sarcomatoid transformation cannot be excluded by imaging alone).

The inferior margin of the mass abuts the right renal hilum and is in close proximity to the pelvis of the collecting system, though there is no definitive infiltration of the pelvicalyceal system identified. The residual normal right renal parenchyma is markedly displaced but discernible at the lower pole.

GEROTA FASCIA AND PERINEPHRIC FAT:
The Gerota fascia appears intact and well-defined around the entirety of the right kidney and renal mass. There is no imaging evidence of direct tumor transgression through the Gerota fascia. The perinephric fat immediately surrounding the mass demonstrates mild stranding and heterogeneity, which may reflect reactive inflammatory change related to the large adjacent mass or early perirenal fat infiltration; however, no discrete nodular deposits or contiguous tumor spread are identified beyond Gerota fascia. This finding is indeterminate and should be correlated intraoperatively.

RIGHT RENAL VEIN AND INFERIOR VENA CAVA:
This is the primary focus of the MRI evaluation. The right renal vein is markedly enlarged, measuring up to 1.9 cm in diameter, and is entirely replaced by an expansile, T2 intermediate, T1 isointense intraluminal filling defect that enhances heterogeneously on dynamic post-contrast sequences, consistent with viable venous tumor thrombus. The thrombus originates at the renal hilum and extends along the full length of the right renal vein toward the IVC confluence. On dedicated coronal T2 and post-contrast VIBE sequences, the distal end of the thrombus is clearly visualized terminating at the right renal vein-IVC junction. The thrombus appears to approach the lateral wall of the IVC but does not extend into or expand the IVC lumen. The IVC itself maintains a normal caliber and flow void on gradient echo sequences, and no intraluminal filling defect is identified within the IVC above or at the level of the right renal vein ostium. There is no occlusion of the IVC. The left renal vein appears patent and normal in caliber.

RIGHT ADRENAL GLAND:
The right adrenal gland is identified as a separate structure, distinctly displaced superomedially by the mass. There is a clear fat plane preserved between the adrenal gland and the dominant right renal tumor mass. No adrenal enlargement, nodule, or signal abnormality is identified. The right adrenal gland appears uninvolved.

LEFT KIDNEY:
The left kidney is of normal size, morphology, and signal characteristics with symmetric corticomedullary differentiation. No left renal mass, hydronephrosis, or calculi identified. Normal enhancement pattern.

RETROPERITONEAL LYMPH NODES:
Consistent with prior CT findings, several enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes are identified. A right para-aortic lymph node measures approximately 2.8 cm in short axis and demonstrates heterogeneous T2 signal and peripheral enhancement with central hypointensity, raising concern for metastatic involvement with central necrosis. An aortocaval node measures approximately 1.9 cm and similarly demonstrates heterogeneous signal characteristics. Additional sub-centimeter nodes are present throughout the retroperitoneum. These findings are consistent with regional nodal metastatic disease.

LIVER:
The liver is normal in size with homogeneous signal on all sequences. No focal hepatic lesions are identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. Portal vein patent.

SPLEEN AND PANCREAS:
Spleen normal in size and signal. Pancreas unremarkable without focal lesion or ductal abnormality.

BOWEL AND MESENTERY:
No bowel obstruction. No mesenteric adenopathy beyond the retroperitoneal lymph nodes described above.

BONES AND SOFT TISSUES:
No marrow replacing lesion or cortical destructive process identified within the imaged osseous structures (lumbar spine, sacrum, bilateral iliac wings, and proximal femora). No suspicious osseous abnormality.

IMPRESSION:

1. LARGE RIGHT RENAL CELL CARCINOMA: Markedly heterogeneous right upper and mid pole renal mass measuring approximately 11.4 cm, with extensive central necrosis and viable enhancing peripheral tumor. Imaging characteristics are consistent with the biopsy-proven clear cell histology. The overall morphology is not significantly changed from recent staging CT.

2. RIGHT RENAL VEIN TUMOR THROMBUS WITHOUT IVC EXTENSION: The right renal vein is completely replaced by viable, enhancing tumor thrombus extending to the renal vein-IVC confluence. Critically, the distal extent of the thrombus terminates at the IVC junction without demonstrable intracaval propagation. The IVC remains patent and of normal caliber. This corresponds to a Mayo Clinic venous tumor thrombus level 0-I, with the thrombus confined to the renal vein. This characterization is highly relevant for surgical planning, as IVC thrombectomy does not appear to be required; however, intraoperative assessment remains essential.

3. GEROTA FASCIA INTACT: No imaging evidence of transgression of Gerota fascia. The surgical plane for radical nephrectomy appears favorable.

4. RIGHT ADRENAL GLAND UNINVOLVED: A distinct fat plane separates the right adrenal gland from the tumor, suggesting that the adrenal gland may potentially be spared at the surgeon's discretion, though proximity should be reassessed intraoperatively.

5. RETROPERITONEAL LYMPHADENOPATHY: Enlarged right para-aortic and aortocaval lymph nodes with imaging features concerning for nodal metastatic involvement, concordant with prior CT staging.

6. NO NEW METASTATIC SITES IDENTIFIED WITHIN THE ABDOMEN: No hepatic, osseous, or adrenal metastatic deposits identified within the imaged field of view. Bilateral pulmonary metastases are not fully evaluated on this dedicated abdominal study and were previously characterized on CT chest.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Histologic sections of all three core needle biopsy specimens demonstrate a malignant neoplasm with biphasic morphology.

The predominant component (approximately 80% of the sampled tissue) consists of sheets and nests of polygonal cells with abundant clear to granular cytoplasm separated by a delicate arborizing sinusoidal vascular network, classic for clear cell renal cell carcinoma. The nuclei are enlarged, vesicular, and markedly pleomorphic with conspicuous macronucleoli readily visible at 10x objective magnification, corresponding to WHO/ISUP nuclear grade 4. Occasional nuclear pseudoinclusions are identified. Mitotic figures are readily identified, including atypical forms. Focal areas of coagulative tumor necrosis are present, particularly in specimen B.

The second component (approximately 20% of the sampled tissue, most prominently in specimen C) consists of sheets of highly pleomorphic spindled to epithelioid cells with high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, eosinophilic cytoplasm, and irregular hyperchromatic nuclei. These cells lack the sinusoidal vascular pattern of the conventional clear cell component and show abrupt transition zones from the conventional carcinomatous areas. Mitotic figures are numerous in this region, including atypical mitoses. This morphology is consistent with SARCOMATOID DIFFERENTIATION, representing high-grade dedifferentiation of the carcinoma. No heterologous sarcomatous elements (e.g., osteosarcoma, rhabdomyosarcoma) are identified.

Tumor necrosis is present (geographic and comedo-type in areas).

No normal renal parenchyma is identified in the sampled cores. No lymphovascular invasion is assessable on these limited biopsy specimens.

ANCILLARY STUDIES

IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY:
Performed on formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded tissue from Specimen A (conventional clear cell component) and Specimen C (sarcomatoid component). Appropriate positive and negative controls were performed concurrently and showed expected reactivity.

  PAX8:             POSITIVE (strong, diffuse nuclear staining, >90% of cells in conventional component; retained expression in sarcomatoid component, approximately 40-50% of cells) -- supports renal origin.

  CA-IX (Carbonic Anhydrase IX):   POSITIVE (diffuse membranous staining in conventional clear cell component, pattern consistent with VHL-deficient/HIF-driven expression; membranous staining in >85% of clear cell component cells with "cup-shaped" pattern) -- strongly supportive of clear cell RCC.

  CD10:             POSITIVE (apical/luminal membranous staining in the conventional component, diffuse in approximately 70% of clear cell areas) -- consistent with renal tubular differentiation.

  Vimentin:         POSITIVE (strong, diffuse cytoplasmic staining in both the conventional clear cell component and the sarcomatoid component).

  CK7:              FOCALLY POSITIVE (weak to moderate staining in approximately 15-20% of conventional clear cell cells; more variable in sarcomatoid areas).

  CK AE1/AE3:       FOCALLY POSITIVE in sarcomatoid component (approximately 30% of spindled cells, weak to moderate intensity), supporting epithelial origin of this high-grade dedifferentiated component.

  TFE3:             NEGATIVE (no nuclear staining detected) -- argues against translocation-associated renal cell carcinoma (Xp11.2 translocation RCC).

  TFEB:             NEGATIVE (no nuclear staining detected) -- performed to exclude t(6;11) translocation RCC. Result pending confirmation at time of preliminary report; FINAL RESULT: NEGATIVE.

COMMENT
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This biopsy demonstrates clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) with sarcomatoid differentiation, estimated at approximately 20% of sampled tissue. The sarcomatoid component is defined by high-grade spindle cell morphology arising in the context of a conventional grade 4 ccRCC, without heterologous differentiated elements. In the 2022 WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumors, sarcomatoid differentiation is no longer a separate histologic subtype but is recognized as a high-grade transformation feature that can occur in any renal cell carcinoma subtype; it is an independent adverse prognostic indicator.

The WHO/ISUP grade 4 designation reflects: (1) the presence of macronucleoli visible at low power (10x), fulfilling Grade 4 criteria independent of the sarcomatoid differentiation, and (2) the sarcomatoid component itself, which by definition confers the highest nuclear grade.

The immunohistochemical profile (PAX8+, CA-IX diffuse+, CD10+, vimentin+, TFE3-, HMB-45-) is consistent with clear cell renal cell carcinoma and effectively excludes translocation-associated RCC (Xp11.2 / TFE3), chromophobe RCC, papillary RCC, collecting duct carcinoma, and renal medullary carcinoma. The focal CK7 positivity in the conventional component is a common finding in ccRCC, particularly higher-grade tumors, and does not support a diagnosis of papillary RCC (which is typically diffusely CK7 positive).

PD-L1 enrichment in the sarcomatoid component is consistent with published data showing that sarcomatoid and rhabdoid features in renal cell carcinoma are associated with higher PD-L1 expression and potentially greater benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy. These findings, combined with the high Ki-67 proliferation index, underscore the aggressive biology of this tumor.

Correlation with clinical, radiographic, and additional molecular studies is recommended. Comprehensive genomic profiling results will be reported as a separate addendum.
