Chromosomal copy number analysis reveals a profile consistent with advanced clear cell renal cell carcinoma. No microsatellite instability (MSI-High) was detected. Tumor mutational burden is low.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
ACTIONABLE / CLINICALLY SIGNIFICANT ALTERATIONS
-------------------------------------------------------------------

1. VHL -- p.L89fs*4 (c.265_266delCT)
   Variant Type: Frameshift deletion
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic -- Loss of Function
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 48%
   Exon: 2
   Zygosity: Consistent with biallelic inactivation (heterozygous deletion + 3p LOH)
   Clinical Significance: VHL is the most commonly inactivated tumor suppressor in clear cell RCC, identified in up to 80-90% of sporadic cases. Loss of VHL protein function leads to constitutive stabilization of hypoxia-inducible factors (HIF-1alpha and HIF-2alpha) and subsequent upregulation of VEGF, PDGF, and other pro-angiogenic targets. This frameshift mutation at codon 89 introduces a premature stop codon (p.L89fs*4) predicted to result in a severely truncated, non-functional protein. Biallelic inactivation is confirmed by concurrent chromosome 3p loss encompassing the VHL locus. This alteration is the founding genomic event in this tumor and provides the mechanistic rationale for VEGF-targeted therapies (Sutent, Votrient, axitinib, cabozantinib, bev). Note: No co-occurring HIF2A mutation was detected; HIF2alpha inhibition (belzutifan) would require separate clinical assessment.

2. PBRM1 -- p.Q357* (c.1069C>T)
   Variant Type: Nonsense mutation
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic -- Loss of Function
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 42%
   Exon: 9
   Zygosity: Consistent with biallelic inactivation (3p LOH)
   Clinical Significance: PBRM1 (Polybromo-1, also known as BAF180) encodes a core subunit of the PBAF SWI/SNF chromatin-remodeling complex. PBRM1 is the second most commonly mutated gene in clear cell RCC (mutated in ~40% of cases), functioning as a tumor suppressor whose loss contributes to transcriptional dysregulation and chromatin accessibility changes. This nonsense mutation (p.Q357*) within the second bromodomain of PBRM1 results in a truncated, non-functional protein. Loss-of-function PBRM1 mutations have been associated with response to PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint blockade in clear cell RCC, potentially through enhanced immunogenicity (Miao et al., Science 2018). However, the co-occurrence of BAP1 loss (see below) partially offsets this favorable predictive association and is concordant with higher tumor grade and sarcomatoid features in this specimen.

3. BAP1 -- p.R227* (c.679C>T)
   Variant Type: Nonsense mutation
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic -- Loss of Function
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 39%
   Exon: 7
   Zygosity: Consistent with biallelic inactivation (3p LOH)
   Clinical Significance: BAP1 (BRCA1-Associated Protein 1) encodes a deubiquitinase that functions as a nuclear tumor suppressor. BAP1 is located on chromosome 3p21.1 and is lost in approximately 10-15% of clear cell RCC cases. Notably, BAP1-mutant clear cell RCCs are associated with significantly higher tumor grade (WHO/ISUP grade 3-4), increased propensity for sarcomatoid differentiation, larger tumor size, and inferior clinical outcomes compared to BAP1-wild type tumors. The concurrent loss of both PBRM1 and BAP1 -- while uncommon, given these genes are typically co-deleted from 3p in a mutually exclusive pattern in some studies -- is observed here and is consistent with this tumor's aggressive phenotype (grade 4, 22% sarcomatoid differentiation, extensive tumor necrosis). This finding may serve as a biomarker of aggressive biology and should inform prognosis and management planning. BAP1 loss has also been reported to associate with enhanced sensitivity to mTOR inhibitor-based therapy in some preclinical and clinical datasets.

4. MET -- Amplification (Copy Number: 6.8 copies; Fold-change: ~3.4x relative to ploidy-adjusted normal)
   Variant Type: Copy Number Amplification
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic -- Gain of Function
   Clinical Significance: MET encodes a receptor tyrosine kinase (hepatocyte growth factor receptor) whose amplification drives oncogenic signaling via RAS/ERK, PI3K/AKT, and Stat3 pathways, promoting cell proliferation, survival, invasion, and metastasis. MET amplification is an uncommon but established alteration in advanced RCC and has been associated with VEGF TKI resistance as well as potential sensitivity to MET-targeting agents. Cabozantinib (XL184), a multi-targeted TKI with potent activity against VEGFR2, MET, and AXL, has demonstrated superior efficacy over suni and Afinitor in patients with MET-dysregulated RCC (METEOR trial, CABOSUN trial data). The presence of MET amplification in this specimen provides a rationale for cabozantinib as a preferred component of the therapeutic strategy. Additionally, MET amplification may promote the invasive and metastatic phenotype observed in this tumor (renal vein thrombus, retroperitoneal nodal spread).

-------------------------------------------------------------------
ADDITIONAL VARIANTS OF BIOLOGICAL SIGNIFICANCE
-------------------------------------------------------------------

5. SETD2 -- p.R1625* (c.4873C>T)
   Variant Type: Nonsense mutation
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic -- Loss of Function
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 31%
   Exon: 21
   Clinical Significance: SETD2 encodes the only non-redundant H3K36 trimethyltransferase in mammalian cells. Located at chromosome 3p21.31, SETD2 is co-deleted with VHL, PBRM1, and BAP1 via 3p loss and is additionally mutated somatically in up to 12% of clear cell RCCs. Loss of SETD2 function results in loss of H3K36me3, leading to aberrant transcription, splicing errors, impaired DNA mismatch repair, and genomic instability. SETD2 loss has been implicated in intratumoral heterogeneity and may contribute to clonal evolution and drug resistance. No direct therapeutic implications at this time, but SETD2 loss may enhance sensitivity to certain DNA-damaging agents.

6. KDM5C -- p.Y611* (c.1833C>G)
   Variant Type: Nonsense mutation
   Variant Classification: Likely Pathogenic -- Loss of Function
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 22%
   Exon: 15
   Clinical Significance: KDM5C (also known as JARID1C) is an X-linked histone H3 lysine 4 (H3K4) demethylase implicated in epigenetic regulation of gene transcription. KDM5C mutations are identified in approximately 7-10% of clear cell RCCs and have been reported to contribute to epigenetic dysregulation in this cancer type. The lower VAF (22%) compared to PBRM1 and BAP1 suggests this may represent a subclonal event, potentially arising later in tumor evolution. KDM5C loss may contribute to transcriptional heterogeneity and altered immune microenvironment.

7. CDKN2A / CDKN2B -- Homozygous Deletion (Chromosome 9p21.3)
   Variant Type: Homozygous Copy Number Loss
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic -- Loss of Function (both alleles)
   Clinical Significance: Homozygous deletion of the CDKN2A/CDKN2B locus on chromosome 9p21 results in complete loss of p16(INK4a), p14(ARF), and p15(INK4b), which are critical regulators of the G1/S cell cycle checkpoint and ARF-MDM2-p53 tumor suppressor axis. Homozygous deletion of this locus is associated with aggressive tumor behavior, accelerated cell cycle progression, and resistance to CDK4/6 inhibition. In the context of clear cell RCC, CDKN2A homozygous deletion has been associated with higher-grade tumors and shorter progression-free survival on standard therapy. This deletion may partially explain the aggressive grade 4 phenotype, extensive sarcomatoid differentiation, and rapid clinical course observed in this patient.

8. TP53 -- p.R248W (c.742C>T)
   Variant Type: Missense mutation
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic -- Gain of Function
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 18%
   Exon: 7
   Germline/Somatic: Somatic (germline filtering performed; no germline TP53 alteration identified)
   Clinical Significance: TP53 p.R248W is one of the most well-characterized gain-of-function (GOF) TP53 hotspot mutations, resulting in a dominant negative p53 protein that not only fails to suppress tumor growth but actively promotes oncogenesis through aberrant transcriptional activation of proliferative genes and inhibition of wild-type p53 in neighboring cells. TP53 mutations are relatively uncommon in clear cell RCC (5-10%) but when present are associated with worse outcomes. The subclonal VAF (18%) suggests this may represent a later subclonal event, possibly arising in the sarcomatoid component or during metastatic progression. TP53 GOF mutations may confer resistance to certain therapies and can activate PI3K/AKT and MAPK pathway signaling independent of upstream oncogenic drivers.

9. TERT Promoter -- C228T (chr5:1,295,228 C>T)
   Variant Type: Promoter mutation
   Variant Classification: Pathogenic -- Gain of Function
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 35%
   Clinical Significance: The TERT promoter C228T mutation (also denoted C>T at position -124 relative to the ATG start site, creating a de novo ETS/GABPA transcription factor binding site) results in transcriptional upregulation of telomerase reverse transcriptase, leading to telomere maintenance and replicative immortality. TERT promoter mutations are found across multiple cancer types and are observed in approximately 10-15% of RCCs. Their presence is associated with advanced-stage disease and unfavorable prognosis. In this context, the relatively high VAF (35%) suggests a clonal event. TERT promoter mutations may synergize with CDKN2A deletion in bypassing cellular senescence checkpoints.

COPY NUMBER ANALYSIS
-------------------------------------------------------------------

Whole-genome copy number profiling reveals the following structural alterations:

- Chromosome 3p LOSS (arm-level): Loss encompassing 3p11.2-3p26.3
  - Includes loci: VHL (3p25.3), PBRM1 (3p21.3), BAP1 (3p21.1), SETD2 (3p21.31)
  - This is the canonical chromosome 3p deletion observed in the majority of clear cell RCCs and represents the sentinel event in this tumor's genomic evolution.

- Chromosome 9p LOSS (arm-level): Loss encompassing 9p21.3-9pter
  - Includes CDKN2A/CDKN2B homozygous deletion (confirmed by SNP-based allelic analysis)
  - Also includes IFNA/IFNB gene cluster at 9p21.3 (potential immunological implications)

- Chromosome 14q LOSS (arm-level): Loss encompassing 14q11.1-14q32.33
  - Includes HIF1A (14q23.2) -- note: no activating mutation in HIF1A identified; loss may reflect downstream transcriptional consequences
  - VHL/HIF pathway alterations combined with 14q loss is a common co-occurring pattern in high-grade clear cell RCC

- Chromosome 5q GAIN (arm-level): Gain encompassing 5q11.1-5q35.3
  - 5q gain is one of the most common copy number gains in clear cell RCC and has been associated with upregulation of oncogenes in this region
  - Does not contain known actionable therapeutic targets in this context

- MET (7q31) Focal Amplification: Copy number 6.8; high-level amplification relative to ploidy baseline

- No high-level amplifications in VEGFR1 (FLT1), VEGFR2 (KDR), EGFR, ERBB2, CDK4, CDK6, or MDM2
- No evidence of BRCA1 or BRCA2 copy number loss or mutation

-------------------------------------------------------------------
GENOMIC EVOLUTION AND CLONAL ARCHITECTURE INTERPRETATION
-------------------------------------------------------------------

Integrating VAF data, copy number changes, and known patterns of clear cell RCC evolution, the following clonal structure is inferred:

Clonal (founding) events (high VAF, consistent with 3p LOH copy-neutral or deletion):
  - VHL p.L89fs*4 (VAF 48%) -- founding tumor suppressor event
  - PBRM1 p.Q357* (VAF 42%) -- early subclonal or near-clonal event following VHL loss
  - BAP1 p.R227* (VAF 39%) -- early co-occurring event; co-loss of PBRM1 and BAP1 is unusual and indicates a particularly aggressive genomic evolution
  - TERT promoter C228T (VAF 35%) -- clonal event enabling telomere maintenance and replicative immortality
  - SETD2 p.R1625* (VAF 31%) -- likely clonal; consistent with 3p LOH

Subclonal events (lower VAF, potentially arising later):
  - KDM5C p.Y611* (VAF 22%) -- subclonal; may drive epigenetic heterogeneity
  - TP53 p.R248W (VAF 18%) -- subclonal, possibly enriched in sarcomatoid component
  - CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion -- likely subclonal or arising during aggressive clonal evolution; chromosomal deletion event
  - MET amplification (VAF interpretation limited by CN analysis) -- may represent a late-arising amplification driving the invasive/metastatic phenotype

Overall genomic complexity is high, consistent with a grade 4 tumor with sarcomatoid transformation. The combination of early epigenetic deregulation (VHL, PBRM1, BAP1, SETD2, KDM5C) followed by cell cycle dysregulation (CDKN2A/B deletion), telomere immortalization (TERT promoter), p53 inactivation/GOF, and MET amplification represents a stepwise progression from a clear cell RCC precursor to an aggressive, metastatic carcinoma with sarcomatoid features.

CLINICAL INTERPRETATION NOTE
-------------------------------------------------------------------
This comprehensive genomic profile is consistent with aggressive, high-grade clear cell RCC. The VHL frameshift mutation serves as the canonical initiating event, driving HIF-dependent oncogenesis and supporting the use of anti-angiogenic/VEGFR-targeted therapies. Concurrent BAP1 and PBRM1 loss -- while individually common in clear cell RCC -- together are associated with the aggressive grade 4, sarcomatoid phenotype observed in this patient. MET amplification is of particular therapeutic relevance, given the availability of cabozantinib as a MET/VEGFR/AXL multi-kinase inhibitor with demonstrated efficacy in RCC and possible enhanced benefit in MET-amplified tumors. The MSS, TMB-low profile does not support use of TMB-based immunotherapy indications, though the elevated PD-L1 CPS (18) and PBRM1 loss status may be consistent with benefit from checkpoint inhibitor-containing regimens (e.g., nivo/ipi) in the appropriate clinical context. Germline testing for BAP1 germline mutation syndrome (BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome; associated with uveal melanoma, mesothelioma, and RCC) should be discussed with the patient and genetic counseling offered, given the identified somatic BAP1 loss-of-function mutation -- though current analysis represents somatic sequencing with germline subtraction performed.

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**

Mr. Kowalski is a 63-year-old retired industrial engineer with a 30 pack-year smoking history (quit 15 years ago), hypertension, and type 2 diabetes mellitus. He presented approximately four weeks ago with a three-week history of gross hematuria, right-sided flank pain, and approximately 15 pounds of unintentional weight loss over the preceding three months. Initial work-up by his primary care physician included urinalysis confirming hematuria and a comprehensive metabolic panel notable for mildly elevated serum calcium at 10.9 mg/dL. He was promptly referred to urology for further evaluation.

Cross-sectional imaging was obtained. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast demonstrated a large, heterogeneous right renal mass measuring 11.4 x 9.8 x 10.1 cm arising from the upper and mid pole with central necrosis, right renal vein involvement without definite extension into the inferior vena cava, and multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules (largest 1.4 cm in the right lower lobe) consistent with metastatic disease. Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy was identified, with a 2.8 cm right para-aortic node and a 1.9 cm aortocaval node. No hepatic, osseous, or adrenal metastases were identified on initial staging CT.

Subsequent MRI of the abdomen with and without contrast was performed for pre-surgical planning and confirmed the right renal mass with a renal vein thrombus extending to but not into the inferior vena cava (IVC). No IVC thrombus was identified. Gerota fascia was intact. The right adrenal gland appeared uninvolved. Retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy was confirmed.

Given the appearance of the mass, a CT-guided core needle biopsy of the right renal mass was performed. Final pathology was diagnostic of clear cell renal cell carcinoma, WHO/ISUP grade 4, with sarcomatoid differentiation estimated at 20% of the sampled tissue. Immunohistochemistry was notable for: PAX8 positive, CA-IX diffusely positive, CD10 positive, vimentin positive, CK7 focally positive, TFE3 negative. Ki-67 proliferation index was elevated at 35%, confirming highly proliferative disease. PD-L1 (SP142) combined positive score was 15, tumor proportion score 10%.

Comprehensive genomic profiling (Foundation One CDx) was performed on the biopsy specimen and returned with actionable and co-occurring alterations: VHL p.L89fs*4 (loss of function, VAF 48%), PBRM1 p.Q357* (loss of function, VAF 42%), BAP1 p.R227* (loss of function, VAF 39%), SETD2 p.R1625* (VAF 31%), KDM5C p.Y611* (VAF 22%), CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion, MET amplification (copy number 6.8), TP53 p.R248W (VAF 18%), and a TERT promoter C228T mutation (VAF 35%). Chromosome 3p loss encompassing VHL, PBRM1, BAP1, and SETD2 loci was confirmed on copy number analysis. Tumor mutational burden was low at 6.2 mutations/Mb; microsatellite status was stable (MSS). PD-L1 by 22C3 returned a CPS of 18. No TSC1/TSC2, HIF2A, FGFR, or NF2 alterations were identified. The co-occurring PBRM1 and BAP1 loss was flagged by the reporting laboratory as associated with higher-grade histology and sarcomatoid dedifferentiation, consistent with this patient's pathology.

**MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD DISCUSSION**

The case of Mr. Kowalski was reviewed in detail at today's GU tumor board. Present disciplines: GU Medical Oncology (attending), Urology/Surgical Oncology (attending), Radiation Oncology (attending), Interventional Radiology, and GU Diagnostic Radiology (attending). The following key discussion points were addressed:

1. **Stage and risk stratification**: Stage IV (cT3bN1M1) ccRCC by AJCC 8th edition. IMDC Intermediate risk (2 factors). Biologically high-risk features include grade 4 histology, 20-22% sarcomatoid differentiation, BAP1 loss, and CDKN2A/B deletion. These genomic features are associated with aggressive disease behavior and poor prognosis on historical data but are not yet incorporated into risk-stratification models.

2. **Role of cytoreductive nephrectomy (CN)**: The question of upfront CN versus systemic therapy first was debated. Key considerations: (a) The CARMENA trial showed non-inferiority of Sutent alone vs. CN + suni in IMDC intermediate/poor risk patients; however, this data does not directly apply to the immunotherapy era. (b) This patient has an IMDC intermediate risk score (2 factors), has reasonable performance status (KPS 80, ECOG 1), and has limited metastatic burden -- oligometastatic pulmonary disease and regional retroperitoneal adenopathy. The primary tumor is large and symptomatic (gross hematuria, flank pain). (c) Nephrectomy was felt to offer meaningful tumor burden reduction (primary is the dominant site of disease by volume), symptom palliation (ongoing hematuria, flank discomfort), and definitive pathologic characterization of the sarcomatoid component. (d) The surgical oncology team confirmed technical feasibility; renal vein thrombus is manageable with planned open right radical nephrectomy. IVC is spared. (e) Urology/surgical oncology team recommends open cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with en bloc Gerota fascia excision, right adrenalectomy, and template retroperitoneal lymph node dissection given nodal involvement. The board consensus is in favor of CN as the initial step.

3. **Planned systemic therapy post-CN**: Given IMDC intermediate risk, availability of immunotherapy combination data (CheckMate 214), and the elevated PD-L1 expression on both biopsy (CPS 15) and NGS assay (CPS 18 by 22C3), and particularly given the sarcomatoid differentiation -- a histologic feature shown in the CheckMate 214 subgroup analysis and subsequently confirmed in broader analyses to derive the most robust and durable benefit from Yervoy plus nivo compared to Sutent -- the board strongly favors Yervoy (ipi) 1 mg/kg + Opdivo (nivo) 3 mg/kg IV every three weeks for four induction cycles, followed by nivo 480 mg flat dosing every four weeks as maintenance. This regimen (ipi/nivo, the Opdivo-Yervoy combination in RCC) has demonstrated superior OS, PFS, and ORR over Sutent in IMDC intermediate/poor risk patients, with the sarcomatoid subgroup showing particularly compelling responses (ORR ~57% in sarcomatoid component data). The MET amplification on NGS is noted; though not a primary driver of treatment selection in the first-line setting, it is flagged as potentially clinically relevant if second-line VEGFR/MET inhibitor therapy (e.g., cabozantinib, which inhibits VEGFR2, MET, and AXL) is ultimately required.

4. **Role of radiation**: Radiation oncology reviewed the case. No role for upfront radiotherapy identified. Pulmonary nodules are small and not causing symptoms. No CNS involvement. Radiation is held in reserve for palliation or stereotactic body radiotherapy (SBRT) if oligoprogression develops after systemic therapy.

5. **Bone health and ancillary assessments**: No evidence of osseous metastases on current staging CT. Bone-directed therapy not indicated at this time. Endocrine evaluation and diabetes management optimization recommended pre-surgery (metformin should be held perioperatively per standard protocol given renal implications). HbA1c pending.

**Assessment**: Mr. Kowalski is a 63-year-old male with newly diagnosed stage IV (cT3bN1M1) clear cell renal cell carcinoma, IMDC intermediate risk (2 factors), WHO/ISUP grade 4 with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation, PD-L1 positive (CPS 18 by 22C3), with VHL/PBRM1/BAP1 co-mutation, MET amplification, and a high-risk molecular profile. 1. **Cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection**: Recommended by multidisciplinary consensus. Open approach planned by urology/surgical oncology. Anticipated R0 resection of primary. Renal vein thrombus to be excised. Right adrenalectomy included. 6. **Next medical oncology visit**: Scheduled approximately 4 weeks post-nephrectomy for post-surgical assessment, repeat laboratory evaluation (CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH baseline prior to immunotherapy initiation), and formal treatment consent for ipi/nivo.

7. **Tumor board documentation**: Case formally entered into the GU multidisciplinary tumor board registry. Consensus recommendation documented for cytoreductive nephrectomy followed by ipi/Opdivo induction and nivo maintenance.

SPECIMEN A -- RIGHT RADICAL NEPHRECTOMY:

PRIMARY TUMOR: Sections confirm a high-grade clear cell renal cell carcinoma with a predominantly alveolar/nested architecture and areas of solid sheet-like growth, punctuated by extensive geographic necrosis comprising greater than 50% of the total tumor cross-sectional area. The clear cell component demonstrates cells with abundant pale to vacuolated cytoplasm and eccentrically placed nuclei; the highest-grade conventional clear cell areas show prominent, irregular, cherry-red macronucleoli readily visible at 10x objective, consistent with WHO/ISUP grade 4. Nuclear pleomorphism is marked, with multinucleated and bizarre giant tumor cells scattered throughout.

SARCOMATOID DIFFERENTIATION: The densely cellular, spindle-cell component identified grossly is confirmed on microscopic examination. This sarcomatoid component consists of elongated to ovoid cells with markedly pleomorphic hyperchromatic nuclei, conspicuous nucleoli, scant-to-moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm, and a high mitotic rate (up to 11 mitoses per 10 high-power fields, including atypical forms). The sarcomatoid foci are arranged in fascicles and storiform patterns without convincing differentiation toward a specific heterologous element. The sarcomatoid component is estimated to comprise 22% of the viable tumor volume on systematic sampling. Transition zones between the conventional clear cell component and the sarcomatoid component are identified. Rhabdoid differentiation is not identified. No heterologous differentiation (e.g., osteosarcomatous, chondrosarcomatous, or rhabdomyosarcomatous elements) is present.

LYMPHOVASCULAR INVASION: Present. Numerous lymphovascular spaces within and at the periphery of the tumor are involved by tumor emboli.

COMMENT
------------------------------------------------------------------------
This nephrectomy specimen confirms the pre-operative biopsy findings of high-grade clear cell renal cell carcinoma with sarcomatoid differentiation. The sarcomatoid component has increased very slightly from the biopsy estimate of 20% to 22% on whole-specimen systematic sampling, consistent with sampling variability. The extensive coagulative necrosis (>50%) and the elevated Ki-67 proliferation index within the sarcomatoid component are consistent with highly aggressive tumor biology. Co-expression of both PAX8 positivity (focal) in the sarcomatoid areas and diffuse CA-IX positivity in the conventional component supports the interpretation of sarcomatoid transformation of a clear cell renal cell carcinoma rather than a primary renal sarcoma. The detection of elevated PD-L1 expression (CPS 22) particularly in the sarcomatoid component is consistent with prior comprehensive genomic profiling (BAP1 loss, which has been associated with sarcomatoid differentiation and higher PD-L1 expression in ccRCC). The positive vascular (renal vein) resection margin reflects the extent of venous thrombus identified pre-operatively and intraoperatively; this finding has been communicated to the surgical team. Extranodal extension in the largest metastatic lymph node is noted. TFE3 FISH results will be addended upon receipt.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Kowalski is a 63-year-old male with metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma, WHO/ISUP grade 4, with significant sarcomatoid differentiation (22%), IMDC intermediate risk (2 factors), who is now 4 weeks post cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy and presenting for initiation of systemic immunotherapy. His post-operative recovery has been unremarkable with resolution of presenting symptoms (hematuria, flank pain), normalization of his paraneoplastic hypercalcemia and thrombocytosis, and adequate performance status.

1. METASTATIC CLEAR CELL RENAL CELL CARCINOMA -- SYSTEMIC THERAPY INITIATION

The multidisciplinary tumor board recommendation, which I endorse, is to proceed with first-line combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab (Yervoy) 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab (Opdivo) 3 mg/kg intravenously every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles, followed by nivolumab 480 mg flat dose IV every 4 weeks as maintenance therapy.

Rationale for this regimen:
- CheckMate-214 established ipilimumab plus nivolumab as a standard first-line option for metastatic clear cell RCC, with superior overall survival compared to sunitinib in intermediate- and poor-risk patients. The 5-year follow-up data continue to show a meaningful survival tail in approximately 25-30% of patients, suggesting potential for durable, long-term disease control.
- His tumor's pathologic and molecular profile strongly supports immunotherapy: WHO/ISUP grade 4 disease with sarcomatoid differentiation is known to be highly enriched for PD-L1 expression and has demonstrated particularly robust responses to dual checkpoint blockade -- a prospective analysis within CheckMate-214 showed an objective response rate of approximately 56% in sarcomatoid RCC subsets with ipi/nivo, compared to approximately 19% with sunitinib. His PD-L1 CPS of 18-22 (depending on assay used) further supports anticipated immunotherapy sensitivity.
- BAP1 loss, identified on his NGS, has been associated with higher-grade clear cell RCC and sarcomatoid features; although no specific approved targeted therapy exists for BAP1-deficient RCC, the association with immune-rich tumor biology further supports checkpoint inhibitor-based therapy.
- MET amplification (copy number 6.8) on NGS is noted -- this may be relevant for second-line TKI selection if progression were to occur, as cabozantinib has potent activity against both VEGFR and MET. This will be kept in mind for future treatment planning.
- IMDC intermediate risk patients derive meaningful benefit from ipi/nivo and represent the core population from CheckMate-214.

Treatment plan details:
- Ipilimumab 1 mg/kg IV over 30 minutes, immediately followed by nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV over 30 minutes, every 21 days x 4 cycles (induction)
- Following cycle 4: nivolumab 480 mg IV flat dose every 28 days (maintenance monotherapy)
- Premedications: diphenhydramine 25 mg IV and acetaminophen 650 mg PO prior to each infusion
- Labs prior to each cycle: CBC with differential, CMP (including LFTs, creatinine, electrolytes), TSH every other cycle
- Baseline TSH documented today as 2.1 mIU/L (normal)
- Echocardiogram not routinely required but will be ordered if cardiac symptoms develop

First cycle scheduled to be administered in the infusion center this week.

2. IMMUNE-RELATED ADVERSE EVENT (irAE) COUNSELING

The patient and his wife received extensive counseling today regarding the spectrum of immune-related adverse events that can occur with dual PD-1/CTLA-4 blockade. Key points covered:

- Endocrinopathies: hypothyroidism (most common), hyperthyroidism (less common, can precede hypothyroidism), hypophysitis (pituitary inflammation -- can cause fatigue, headache, and hormonal deficiencies), adrenal insufficiency (potentially life-threatening -- must report severe fatigue, nausea, hypotension), type 1 diabetes (rare)
- Colitis: immune-mediated diarrhea/colitis is one of the most common grade 3/4 events with CTLA-4 blockade; patient instructed to report any significant increase in bowel frequency, abdominal cramping, or bloody stool immediately
- Hepatitis: asymptomatic transaminase elevation most common; LFTs monitored at each cycle
- Pneumonitis: immune-mediated inflammation of the lung; report any new or worsening shortness of breath, cough, or hypoxia -- particularly relevant given his pulmonary metastases making clinical monitoring more challenging
- Dermatitis: rash is common; usually manageable; severe blistering or mucous membrane involvement requires prompt attention
- Nephritis: report decreased urine output or significant edema (also need to monitor creatinine closely given post-nephrectomy CKD)
- Neurotoxicity: rare but serious; report any new weakness, numbness, double vision, or cognitive changes
- Myocarditis: rare but potentially life-threatening; report chest pain, palpitations, or syncope immediately
- Arthritis/myositis: joint pains and muscle inflammation can occur

Patient given written irAE information sheet and oncology nurse contact phone number for urgent concerns. He verbalized understanding of when to seek immediate medical attention versus routine reporting at next visit. Instructed NOT to self-administer steroids or stop treatment without contacting our team.

In general, grade 1-2 endocrinopathies are managed with hormone replacement and continuation of therapy; grade 2 colitis, pneumonitis, or hepatitis typically requires treatment hold and oral steroids; grade 3-4 events require high-dose IV steroids and likely permanent discontinuation of the offending agent. Given his dual combination therapy, the rate of grade 3/4 irAEs is approximately 25-35% vs. ~17% with single-agent nivolumab.

3. POST-NEPHRECTOMY RENAL FUNCTION

Creatinine of 1.6 mg/dL (eGFR approximately 46 mL/min/1.73m2) represents expected post-nephrectomy decline from his pre-operative baseline of 1.1 mg/dL. This is consistent with normal physiologic adaptation to unilateral nephrectomy. His creatinine should stabilize or modestly improve over the next several months as hypertrophic adaptation of the remaining left kidney occurs. Checkpoint inhibitors are generally well-tolerated in patients with CKD; however, immune-mediated nephritis (tubulointerstitial nephritis) is a recognized irAE that would need to be distinguished from progressive CKD. Baseline creatinine documented today. Metformin continued at current dose (eGFR > 45 mL/min/1.73m2); will reassess if creatinine worsens. Lisinopril continued for both hypertension and renoprotection; blood pressure is well-controlled at 136/84 today.

4. ANEMIA

Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, recovering from post-operative blood loss. Ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily continued. Reticulocyte count not checked today but anemia is consistent with post-surgical etiology. Will recheck CBC at cycle 2. Transfusion threshold approximately 7-8 g/dL unless symptomatic. Patient has mild exertional dyspnea consistent with anemia and deconditioning; this should improve with recovery.

5. DIABETES MANAGEMENT

HbA1c 7.1% -- acceptable management. Glucose 114 mg/dL today. Metformin continued as above. Particular vigilance will be maintained for immune checkpoint inhibitor-associated type 1 diabetes (very rare but can present as fulminant DKA). Patient instructed to report markedly elevated blood sugars, excessive thirst, polyuria, nausea, or vomiting. Blood glucose monitoring advised at home during treatment.

6. HYPERTENSION

BP 136/84 mmHg today -- well-controlled on lisinopril 10 mg daily. No medication changes at this time.

7. HYPOTHYROIDISM SCREENING

Baseline TSH normal. Thyroid function will be checked every other cycle (every 6 weeks during induction). Patient counseled that hypothyroidism is the most common endocrine irAE with this regimen and is readily managed with levothyroxine replacement, which does not require dose modification or treatment interruption.

8. PERFORMANCE STATUS

KPS 80, ECOG 1. Patient is ambulatory and capable of all self-care. Recovering well from major abdominal surgery. No contraindication to initiating systemic therapy at this time. He will be reassessed at each cycle.

9. FOLLOW-UP AND MONITORING

- Cycle 1 of ipilimumab 1 mg/kg + nivolumab 3 mg/kg to be administered in infusion center this week
- Labs prior to each cycle (CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH q2 cycles)
- Telephone check-in by oncology nurse at 1 week after first infusion to assess for early irAEs
- Next clinic visit in 3 weeks (prior to cycle 2)
- Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis after completing 4 induction cycles (approximately 3 months from today)
- PCP to co-manage diabetes and hypertension; communication sent today
- Referral to oncology nutrition and survivorship clinic placed

Time spent with patient and family today: approximately 65 minutes, predominantly in counseling, education, and discussion of the treatment plan, risks, benefits, and alternatives.
